channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: Using Digital Twin Methods to Accurately Simulate Quantum Error Correction
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a promising approach to one of quantum computing's most pressing challenges: accurately modeling and predicting the behavior of quantum error correction codes in real hardware environments. By applying digital twin methodology—creating detailed virtual replicas that mirror physical systems—researchers can better simulate the complex interactions and crosstalk effects that occur in actual quantum processors, moving beyond idealized theoretical models. This work represents a significant step toward practical quantum error correction, as it allows engineers to test and optimize error correction strategies before implementing them on expensive quantum hardware. The ability to accurately predict how surface codes will perform under realistic conditions could accelerate the timeline for achieving fault-tolerant quantum computers capable of running useful algorithms.

Diagram of a distance‑7 rotated surface code with 97 physical qubits (49 data qubits + 48 measurement qubits) modelled by the team. Grey nodes are data qubits; labeled circles are ancillas measuring X- or Z-type checks on neighboring data qubits. Edge colors encode the residual ZZ crosstalk strength (darker edges indicate stronger crosstalk).Credit: Quantum Elements [...] The post Using Digital Twin Methods to Accurately Simulate Quantum Error Correction appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The Decryption Threshold — Re-estimating the Quantum Threat to Blockchain Infrastructure
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum computing's most pressing questions: when will quantum computers actually threaten current cryptographic systems like those protecting blockchain networks? Recent estimates from Google Quantum AI and Oratomic are refining our understanding of the quantum resources needed to break 256-bit elliptic curve cryptography, which secures Bitcoin and many other cryptocurrencies. These updated calculations are crucial for determining realistic timelines for quantum advantage in cryptography and help inform when organizations need to transition to quantum-resistant security protocols. The work represents a more nuanced view of the quantum threat, moving beyond theoretical possibilities to practical resource requirements that consider real-world constraints like error rates and logical qubit overhead.

Comparison of logical quantum resources (number of logical qubits and Toffoli gates) required to break 256-bit ECDLP for the secp256k1 curve, as reported by various prior works. Credit: Google Quantum AI whitepaper. By Mohamed Abdel-Kareem Recent technical disclosures from Google Quantum AI and the newly emerged Oratomic have provided updated resource estimates for executing Shor’s [...] The post The Decryption Threshold — Re-estimating the Quantum Threat to Blockchain Infrastructure appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Palm Beach State College Takes Aim at Quantum Workforce Demand
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article addresses a critical bottleneck in quantum computing's transition from research labs to commercial reality: the growing skills gap between current academic-focused talent pipelines and the diverse workforce needed for practical quantum applications. As quantum technologies mature beyond pure research into engineering, manufacturing, and operations roles, community colleges like Palm Beach State are pioneering accessible pathways that don't require advanced degrees, potentially democratizing entry into the field. This workforce development initiative represents a crucial step toward scaling quantum technology deployment, since even the most breakthrough quantum systems will need technicians, operators, and support staff who understand the fundamentals but don't necessarily need PhD-level expertise. The success of such programs could determine whether quantum computing remains an elite academic pursuit or becomes a transformative industry with broad economic impact.

Insider Brief Quantum technology is often portrayed as a field staffed by people with doctoral degrees and years of academic experience. That may be how it is now, but experts are increasingly concerned that as quantum moves out of theory and into practice and out of the laboratory and into the real world, its success […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: SpinQ Technology Secures Nearly 1 Billion Chinese Yuan in Series C Funding to Scale Industrial Superconducting Quantum Computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This significant funding milestone positions SpinQ as one of the best-capitalized quantum computing companies globally, particularly notable given their focus on superconducting quantum systems - the same approach used by IBM and Google. With nearly $139 million in Series C funding alone, SpinQ is demonstrating that serious quantum hardware development isn't limited to Western companies, as China continues building its quantum computing ecosystem with substantial private investment backing. The company's "full-stack" approach, covering everything from hardware to software applications, suggests they're positioning themselves as a comprehensive quantum solutions provider rather than focusing on a single component of the quantum computing stack. This level of funding will be crucial for scaling up manufacturing and competing in the increasingly expensive race to build fault-tolerant quantum computers.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — SpinQ Technology, a leading global provider of full-stack quantum computing solutions, today announced the successful completion of its 600 million Chinese Yuan (about $83 million U.S.) Series C+ financing round. This milestone brings the company’s total Series C funding to nearly 1 billion Chinese Yuan (about $139 million U.S.) within […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Coinbase CEO Addresses ‘Quantum Threat’ to Cryptocurrencies
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement signals a significant shift in how the cryptocurrency industry views quantum computing timelines, moving from dismissive skepticism to active preparation as quantum error correction milestones accelerate. Armstrong's decision to personally spearhead quantum-resistant upgrades suggests that recent breakthroughs in logical qubit demonstrations and error correction progress have finally convinced crypto leaders that the threat is becoming tangible rather than theoretical. The timing is particularly noteworthy given the flurry of quantum error correction announcements from major players like Google, IBM, and others throughout 2024, which appear to have created genuine urgency in an industry that previously viewed quantum computers as decades away from practical relevance. This represents one of the clearest real-world examples of quantum computing progress driving immediate changes in billion-dollar industries, even before fault-tolerant quantum computers actually exist.

Insider Brief A steady stream of announcements that quantum teams are making progress on quantum error correction seems to have stirred the once-skeptical cryptocurrency community. Coinbase CEO and co-founder Brian Armstrong said he will personally lead an industry push to make Bitcoin quantum-resistant, marking a shift from long-term concern to near-term engineering priority after new […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuEra Launches Open-Source Package to Simulate Logical Quantum Circuits at Scale
item.description:
Curator's Take: This open-source release represents a crucial step forward in quantum error correction research, as simulating non-Clifford gates like T-gates has historically been computationally expensive and limited researchers' ability to test large-scale fault-tolerant quantum algorithms. QuEra's GPU-accelerated simulator addresses a critical bottleneck by enabling researchers to model the complete gate set needed for universal quantum computing at the scales required for meaningful quantum error correction development. The timing is particularly significant as the quantum computing field rapidly approaches the threshold where logical qubits become practical, making tools that can simulate these error-corrected systems essential for algorithm development and hardware design. By open-sourcing this capability, QuEra is democratizing access to advanced quantum simulation tools that were previously available only to well-funded research groups with specialized computational resources.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QuEra Computing today open-sourced its T-gate simulator (Tsim), a GPU-accelerated quantum circuit simulator that, for the first time, lets researchers simulate non-Clifford gate operations at the speed and scale that quantum error correction (QEC) development demands. QEC is the essential bridge between today’s noisy quantum processors and the fault-tolerant machines […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum entanglement between electrons and ions captured at attosecond timescale
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a remarkable advance in our understanding of quantum entanglement by capturing these mysterious correlations at the fastest timescales ever observed - the attosecond realm where electrons move around atomic nuclei. The ability to observe entanglement between electrons and ions at such ultrafast timescales opens new possibilities for studying fundamental quantum phenomena that occur during the earliest moments of quantum interactions. This research could prove crucial for developing next-generation quantum technologies, as understanding how entanglement forms and evolves at these speeds may help scientists design more robust quantum systems and potentially discover new ways to manipulate quantum states for computing and communication applications.

Quantum mechanics is extremely successful at describing the behavior of matter at the atomic level. This success forces one to accept that certain aspects of physical reality go far beyond our intuition. Among these, none is more intriguing than the concept of quantum entanglement, which mathematically describes how two particles that have at some point in the past interacted with each other retain a memory of this interaction to such an extent that acting on one of the two particles has a measurable influence on the properties of the other particle, even if the two have long ago stopped interacting and may be separated by such a vast distance that communication between them is no longer possible.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Space-Efficient Quantum Algorithm for Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithms with Resource Estimation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most pressing questions in quantum computing: exactly how many logical qubits will be needed to break real-world elliptic curve cryptography using Shor's algorithm. By developing a more space-efficient approach to modular inversion, the researchers have significantly reduced the qubit requirements for attacking 256-bit elliptic curves from over 2000 to just 1333 logical qubits, bringing quantum cryptanalysis closer to the capabilities of near-term fault-tolerant quantum computers. This work provides crucial benchmarks for both cryptographers assessing when current encryption standards might become vulnerable and quantum hardware developers planning the specifications for future quantum computers. The reduction represents meaningful progress toward the threshold where quantum computers could pose a genuine threat to widely-used cryptographic systems that protect everything from online banking to secure communications.

Solving the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP) is critical for evaluating the quantum security of widely deployed elliptic-curve cryptosystems. Consequently, minimizing the number of logical qubits required to execute this algorithm is a key object. In implementations of Shor's algorithm, the space complexity is largely dictated by the modular inversion operation during point addition. Starting from the extended Euclidean algorithm (EEA), we refine the register-sharing method of Proos and Zalka and propose a space-efficient reversible modular inversion algorithm. We use length registers together with location-controlled arithmetic to store the intermediate variables in a compact form throughout the computation. We then optimize the stepwise update rules and give concrete circuit constructions for the resulting controlled arithmetic components. This leads to a modular inversion circuit that uses $3n + 4\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + O(1)$ logical qubits and $204n^2\log_2 n + O(n^2)$ Toffoli gates. By inserting this modular inversion component into the controlled affine point-addition circuit, we obtain a space-efficient algorithm for the ECDLP with $5n + 4\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + O(1)$ qubits and $O(n^3)$ Toffoli gates. In particular, for a 256-bit prime-field curve, our estimate reduces the logical-qubit count to 1333, compared with 2124 in the previous low-width implementation of Häner et al.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuiX Quantum Demonstrates Below-Threshold Error Mitigation in Photonic Hardware
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough marks a critical milestone for photonic quantum computing, as QuiX Quantum has achieved the first demonstration of net-positive error correction in photonic hardware - meaning their error mitigation actually reduces rather than amplifies noise. While superconducting and trapped-ion systems have dominated quantum error correction research, photonic platforms offer unique advantages like room-temperature operation and natural networking capabilities, making this achievement particularly significant for distributed quantum computing applications. The collaboration with NASA's Quantum AI Lab suggests potential applications in space-based quantum systems, where photonic qubits' resilience to environmental interference could prove invaluable. This demonstration brings photonic quantum computers closer to practical utility by proving they can overcome one of quantum computing's fundamental challenges at the hardware level.

QuiX Quantum, in collaboration with NASA’s Quantum Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, the University of Twente, and Freie Universität Berlin, has demonstrated "below-threshold" error mitigation on a photonic quantum computer. The experiment, conducted on the QuiX Bia™ Cloud Quantum Computing Service, represents the first hardware-level verification of net-positive error reduction in a photonic system. By suppressing physical [...] The post QuiX Quantum Demonstrates Below-Threshold Error Mitigation in Photonic Hardware appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Twinning! Quantum ‘Digital Twins’ Tackle Error Correction Task to Speed Path to Reliable Quantum Computers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights an intriguing approach to one of quantum computing's biggest challenges - using classical "digital twins" to model and understand error behavior in quantum systems before building the actual hardware. The timing is particularly notable as it comes during what appears to be a breakthrough week for quantum error correction research, suggesting the field is rapidly converging on multiple complementary solutions to the reliability problem. By allowing researchers to test error correction strategies virtually before implementing them on expensive quantum hardware, this classical simulation approach could dramatically accelerate the development cycle for fault-tolerant quantum computers. The collaboration with Amazon Web Services also signals growing industry investment in practical quantum error mitigation tools that bridge the gap between today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and tomorrow's error-corrected quantum computers.

Insider Brief In a week that has seen a parade of error-correction advances, a team of quantum computing researchers say they have shown a practical way to simulate large, error-prone quantum systems on classical computers, an advance that the scientists suggest could speed the path toward reliable quantum machines. A collaboration involving Amazon Web Services, […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Explicit constructions of mutually unbiased bases via Hadamard matrices
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum information theory's most elegant yet stubborn problems: constructing complete sets of mutually unbiased bases, which are fundamental to quantum key distribution, state tomography, and quantum error correction protocols. The authors make significant progress by providing explicit analytical conditions for the notoriously difficult dimension-4 case and developing systematic computational frameworks that bridge pure theory with practical construction methods. What makes this particularly valuable is their focus on the challenging dimension-6 case, where the mathematical constraints are so rigid that complete sets of MUBs may not even exist - a conjecture that has puzzled researchers for decades. This work provides both the theoretical foundations and computational tools needed to advance quantum protocols that rely on these mathematically beautiful but practically crucial structures.

We present a detailed computational and algebraic study of Mutually Unbiased Bases (MUBs) in finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, with a particular focus on dimensions 2, 3, 4, and the challenging case of 6. Starting from the Hadamard-phase parametrization, we derive explicit analytical conditions for mutual unbiasedness in dimension 4, providing a tractable system of trigonometric constraints on the phase parameters. We then explore a tensor-product construction via Pauli operators, highlighting the algebraic and group-theoretical origin of MUBs in two-qubit systems, and demonstrating how these constructions yield a complete set of 5 MUBs in dimension 4. Extending our approach, we investigate the Fourier-family method in dimension 6, where the absence of a prime-power structure imposes strong rigidity constraints and limits the known constructions to sets of 3 MUBs. We provide a systematic computational framework for testing candidate phase vectors, bridging the gap between analytical insight and numerical exploration. Finally, we generalize the discussion to arbitrary prime-power dimensions, emphasizing the role of finite-field structures, Heisenberg-Weyl operators, and discrete symmetries in generating complete sets of MUBs. Our work offers a transparent, line-by-line verification methodology, highlighting both the geometric and algebraic richness of MUBs, and clarifying why certain dimensions resist full analytical constructions. This study serves as a comprehensive resource for researchers seeking both theoretical understanding and practical construction of MUBs in quantum information science.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The Phase Quantum Walk: A Unified Framework for Graph State Distribution in Quantum Networks
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces a clever new approach to one of quantum networking's most fundamental challenges: how to efficiently distribute complex entangled states across multiple quantum processors or nodes. The phase quantum walk framework is particularly elegant because it can create any graph state topology using just simple two-qubit gates, potentially making modular quantum computers much more practical by eliminating the need for direct physical connections between all qubits. The experimental validation on IBM hardware is especially significant, as it provides real-world proof that the theoretical predictions hold true regardless of the specific network topology being created. This could be a game-changer for scaling up quantum networks and distributed quantum computing architectures, where current methods for sharing quantum information between distant processors remain prohibitively complex and error-prone.

Distributing arbitrary graph states across quantum networks is a central challenge for modular quantum computing and measurement-based quantum communication. I introduce the phase quantum walk (PQW), a discrete-time quantum walk in which the conventional position-permuting shift operator is replaced by a diagonal conditional phase (CZ) gate, enabling distribution of arbitrary graph states, not merely GHZ states, from elementary two-qubit resources. The Byproduct Lemma shows that each walk step teleports edge entanglement with a correctable Pauli byproduct; the Coin Invariance Theorem proves that the optimal fidelity F*(C,E) = F*(H,E) for all unitary coins C and noise channels E, with closed-form expressions F_dep = (1 - 3p/4)^k and F_pd = ((1 + sqrt(1 - p))/2)^k. Analytical correction formulas are derived for tree graphs (general theorem) and ring graphs (C4 case study), with F = 1.0 verified across eight topologies (up to 4096 outcomes). Hardware validation on ibm marrakesh (IBM Heron r2, CZ-native) yields F_cl = 0.924 for |GHZ4> and 0.922 for |L4>, statistically identical, providing the first experimental confirmation that fidelity is independent of graph topology as predicted by the Coin Invariance Theorem.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuEra Releases Open-Source GPU-Accelerated T-Gate Simulator for Error Correction Research
item.description:
Curator's Take: This release addresses a crucial bottleneck in quantum error correction research by providing researchers with a specialized tool for simulating T-gates, the non-Clifford operations that make quantum computing universal but are notoriously difficult to implement fault-tolerantly. While existing simulators like STIM excel at Clifford gate operations, QuEra's GPU-accelerated Tsim fills the gap for modeling the statistical behavior of these more complex gates at scale, which is essential for developing practical error correction protocols. The open-source nature of this tool could accelerate progress across the quantum computing community, as researchers can now run high-speed simulations to test and optimize error correction schemes that involve the full universal gate set rather than just the more limited Clifford operations.

QuEra Computing has released Tsim, an open-source, GPU-accelerated quantum circuit simulator designed to model non-Clifford gate operations. The tool addresses a technical gap in the development of quantum error correction (QEC) protocols, which require high-speed, large-scale statistical analysis of universal quantum circuits. While established simulators such as STIM focus on Clifford gates, Tsim provides the [...] The post QuEra Releases Open-Source GPU-Accelerated T-Gate Simulator for Error Correction Research appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quanscient and Haiqu Demonstrate Algorithm for Scalable Computational Nonlinear Fluid Simulations
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration between Quanscient and Haiqu represents a significant milestone in applying quantum computing to real-world engineering problems, successfully demonstrating the most complex quantum fluid dynamics simulation to date on IBM's Heron processor. The 15-step nonlinear fluid benchmark with obstacle interactions showcases how Quantum Lattice Boltzmann Methods could eventually revolutionize computational fluid dynamics, a critical tool used across industries from aerospace design to climate modeling. While current quantum hardware limitations mean this won't replace classical CFD simulations anytime soon, the work provides crucial proof-of-concept evidence that quantum algorithms can handle the mathematical complexity of fluid turbulence and nonlinear interactions. This achievement opens the door for future quantum advantage in solving some of the most computationally demanding problems in engineering and physics.

Quanscient and Haiqu have announced a new quantum algorithm designed to accelerate Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations. The researchers successfully executed a 15-step nonlinear fluid benchmark involving an obstacle, which currently stands as the most physically complex hardware demonstration of a Quantum Lattice Boltzmann Method (QLBM). The benchmark was conducted on the IBM Heron R3 [...] The post Quanscient and Haiqu Demonstrate Algorithm for Scalable Computational Nonlinear Fluid Simulations appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Constrained Quantum Optimization via Iterative Warm-Start XY-Mixers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles one of QAOA's most persistent headaches: how to efficiently handle hard constraints while still benefiting from warm-starting techniques that bias the search toward promising solutions. The researchers cleverly solve a fundamental compatibility issue between XY-mixers (which keep quantum states within feasible regions) and warm-starting by designing new mixer Hamiltonians that maintain essential mathematical properties while incorporating prior knowledge. Their Iterative Warm-Starting approach shows dramatic improvements in numerical simulations, boosting the probability of finding optimal solutions by orders of magnitude on classic problems like Max-k-Cut and the Traveling Salesperson Problem. This represents a significant step toward making QAOA more practical for real-world constrained optimization problems, especially important as we push hybrid algorithms on today's noisy quantum devices.

The Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) is a leading hybrid heuristic for combinatorial optimization, but efficiently handling hard constraints remains a significant challenge. XY-mixers successfully confine quantum state evolution to a feasible subspace, such as the Hamming-weight-1 sector for one-hot constraints. On the contrary, warm-starting biases the search toward promising regions based on preliminary solutions. Combining these two techniques requires maintaining the essential alignment between the initial state and the mixer Hamiltonian to preserve convergence guarantees. Previous work demonstrated warm-starting with XY-mixers via a biased initial state, but relying only on standard mixer Hamiltonians. Consequently, the initial state is no longer a ground state of the mixer. In this work, we overcome these limitations by formulating a warm-started XY-mixer Hamiltonian for one-hot constraints and proving its ground-state properties. Furthermore, we provide a shallow circuit implementation suitable for NISQ implementations. We embed the warm-starting into a classical heuristic that iteratively updates the bias based on previous samples, called Iterative Warm-Starting (IWS). Extensive numerical simulations on Max-$k$-Cut and Traveling Salesperson Problem instances demonstrate that IWS-QAOA significantly accelerates the solution-finding process, increasing the probability of sampling optimal solutions by orders of magnitude compared to standard XY-QAOA. Finally, we validate our approach on the ibm_boston QPU using hardware-tailored 144-qubit problem instances. By coupling IWS-QAOA with a greedy steepest-descent post-processing strategy to repair infeasible measurements caused by hardware noise, we successfully identify optimal solutions on actual quantum devices.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Photonic qubit encoding interconversion for heterogeneous quantum networking
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles a critical infrastructure challenge for quantum networks: how to connect different quantum computing platforms that encode information in photons using incompatible methods. The researchers demonstrated a clever workaround by converting between polarization-based and time-bin encoding schemes, allowing quantum information to travel reliably through standard optical fibers that would otherwise scramble polarization states. This interconversion protocol is essential for building heterogeneous quantum networks where different types of quantum computers, sensors, and communication devices need to share entangled photons despite using different encoding standards. The work represents an important step toward the quantum internet, where diverse quantum technologies will need seamless integration rather than being isolated in their own encoding silos.

Quantum information processing, communication, and sensing networks are being developed with various qubit platforms that use different encoding schemes. Connecting quantum network nodes to distribute entanglement requires matching photon qubit basis encoding. In this work, we implement an interconversion protocol which converts photon qubit encoding from the polarization basis to the time-bin basis, transmits the photons through a transport fiber with large fluctuations in polarization, and converts back to polarization encoding for ease of measurement. This interconversion scheme faithfully transmits a polarization Bell state across the transport fiber by converting sources of infidelity to changes in transmission rate. These results illustrate a practical approach for interfacing distinct qubit platforms to enable modular and flexible operation in heterogeneous quantum networks.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Emergence of volume-law scaling for entanglement negativity from the Hawking radiation of analogue black holes
item.description:
Curator's Take: This groundbreaking work provides the first concrete demonstration that Hawking radiation from analogue black holes creates a fundamentally new type of quantum entanglement scaling that could be observed in current cold-atom experiments. The researchers discovered that logarithmic negativity—a key measure of quantum entanglement—transitions from its typical logarithmic behavior to a volume-law scaling when Hawking radiation is present, directly revealing how entangled particle pairs are distributed across the black hole's interior and exterior regions. This represents a major advance in using laboratory quantum simulators to probe one of physics' deepest mysteries: whether information is truly lost when black holes evaporate, potentially offering experimental insights into questions that have puzzled theorists for decades. The work bridges cutting-edge quantum simulation capabilities with fundamental questions about spacetime, information theory, and the fate of matter that falls into black holes.

The quantum information content of Hawking radiation holds the key to understanding black-hole evaporation and the fate of unitarity. Motivated by recent advances in cold-atom experiments, we develop a lattice-regularization approach aimed at simulating the coarse-grained entanglement scaling of a quantum field in a 1+1D analogue black-hole background. We provide the first concrete demonstration that logarithmic negativity -- an entanglement monotone that typically exhibits a UV-divergent log-scaling for the conformal vacuum -- acquires a UV-finite volume term from the nonlocal correlations seeded by Hawking radiation. We show that this volume term encodes both the number density and spatial distribution of entangled Hawking pairs along the black-hole interior and exterior. We highlight its prospective detection in currently realizable experiments as well as its implications beyond the analogue paradigm, in particular for black-hole thermodynamics.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Decoding Realistic Quantum Error Syndrome with Quantum Elements Digital Twins
item.description:
Curator's Take: This AWS research tackles one of quantum computing's most critical engineering challenges: determining the exact hardware specifications and code sizes needed to achieve practical fault-tolerant quantum computers. By creating digital twins that simulate realistic error conditions, the work moves beyond theoretical thresholds to provide concrete guidance for building quantum systems that can actually run useful algorithms. The approach represents a crucial bridge between quantum error correction theory and the messy realities of physical quantum hardware, potentially accelerating the timeline for commercially viable quantum computers. This type of practical engineering analysis is essential as the field transitions from proof-of-concept demonstrations to building quantum systems that can solve real-world problems.

Fault-tolerant quantum computing requires quantum error correction (QEC): Encoding one logical qubit into many physical qubits so that, below a threshold error rate, the logical error rate falls rapidly as the code grows. The practical engineering question is: How large must the code be and how good must the hardware be to reach a useful […]

Read original at AWS Quantum Computing Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum search algorithm for similar subgraph identification under fixed edge removal
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a quantum algorithm that tackles the challenging problem of finding similar subgraphs when some edges are removed, achieving a polynomial speedup over classical methods through clever use of Dicke states and amplitude estimation. The work is particularly intriguing because it addresses a real-world application in power grid analysis, where understanding network resilience under edge failures is crucial for infrastructure reliability. While the quantum advantage is significant in theory, the practical impact will depend on whether near-term quantum devices can handle the coherence requirements for meaningful problem sizes, making this an important benchmark for tracking quantum computing's progression toward practical utility in network optimization problems.

We introduce a novel quantum algorithm for similar subgraph identification in form of an NP-hard cardinality-constrained binary quadratic optimization problem. Given a weighted reference graph with Laplacian $\boldsymbol{B}$, our algorithm determines the subgraph featuring Laplacian $\boldsymbol{B'}$ on the same vertex set, but $x$ out of $N$ inactive edges, minimizing the Frobenius distance $||\boldsymbol{B} - \boldsymbol{B'}||_\mathrm{F}^2$. We represent the $\binom{N}{x}$ graph topologies by an equal-weight superposition in form of a Dicke state, enabling controlled transformations applied to the quantum state associated with the vectorized Laplacian of the reference graph. Combined with amplitude estimation and a minimum finding approach, our algorithm provides a polynomial speed up $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{N^{x}/x!}N\log\log N)$ compared to $\mathcal{O}(N^{x+1}/x!)$ of classical brute-force search algorithms. We demonstrate the application of our method on standard test cases, which represent electric power grids, by reconstructing $||\boldsymbol{B} -\boldsymbol{B'}||_\mathrm{F}^2$ from measurements and show how our approach can be additionally used to calculate energy functional like quadratic forms of the Laplacians with respect to a given vector.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Dancing to Invisible Choreography, Quantum Computers Can Balance The Noise
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: quantum noise, the random errors that plague quantum systems and prevent them from scaling to useful sizes. While the headline's "dancing to invisible choreography" metaphor is poetic, the underlying issue is deeply practical - current quantum computers lose their quantum properties so quickly due to environmental interference that they can only perform limited calculations before errors overwhelm the results. The piece likely explores emerging error correction techniques and noise mitigation strategies that researchers are developing to build the fault-tolerant quantum computers needed for breakthrough applications in cryptography, drug discovery, and optimization. Understanding and conquering quantum noise represents the difference between today's experimental quantum devices and tomorrow's transformative quantum computers that could revolutionize entire industries.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Large-scale quantum computers are waiting in the wings. One of the main reasons we don’t have them yet is because quantum hardware is so noisy. This isn’t the type of noise you’d want to shush in a crowded theater. When it comes to computers, noise means errors that crop up […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Infleqtion Delivers Quantum Precision Timing for Mission-Critical Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement marks a significant milestone in quantum sensing's transition from laboratory curiosity to real-world infrastructure, as Infleqtion and Safran bring quantum-enhanced timing technology to mission-critical applications. Precision timing is the invisible backbone of everything from GPS navigation to financial trading systems, and quantum sensors can potentially offer unprecedented accuracy and resilience against interference or jamming. The partnership between a quantum startup and an established defense contractor like Safran signals growing confidence that quantum sensing technologies are ready for deployment in high-stakes environments where failure isn't an option. This could represent one of the first major commercial successes for practical quantum technology outside of computing applications.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Infleqtion (NYSE: INFQ), a global leader in quantum sensing and quantum computing powered by neutral-atom technology, announced availability of the first quantum-enabled precision timing solution delivered as part of the company’s partnership with global civil and military equipment manufacturer Safran Electronics & Defense. The new solution includes Infleqtion’s Tiqker™ quantum optical […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuiX Quantum Demonstrates Below-Threshold Error Mitigation in Photonic Quantum Computing For First Time
item.description:
Curator's Take: This achievement represents a crucial milestone for photonic quantum computing, as QuiX Quantum has demonstrated error rates low enough to potentially support fault-tolerant quantum computation using light-based qubits. While IBM and Google have made headlines with similar error mitigation breakthroughs using superconducting systems, this marks the first time a photonic platform has crossed this critical threshold, validating an alternative approach that could offer advantages in networking and room-temperature operation. The demonstration suggests that multiple quantum computing architectures may be viable paths to practical, error-corrected quantum computers, potentially accelerating the field's progress through healthy technological competition. For Europe's quantum ambitions, this represents a significant technical achievement that could position the region as a major player in the global quantum race.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QuiX Quantum, a leading provider of photonic quantum computing hardware, today announced it has demonstrated “below threshold” error mitigation for the first time on a photonic quantum computer, suppressing physical qubit errors to the level compatible with scalable, fault‑tolerant quantum computing. The achievement marks the first time a European company […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Towards Chemically Accurate and Scalable Quantum Simulations on IQM Quantum Hardware: A Quantum-HPC Hybrid Approach
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents a significant step toward practically useful quantum chemistry simulations, demonstrating that current quantum hardware can tackle real molecular problems beyond simple benchmark systems. The researchers' hybrid approach combining quantum computing with classical high-performance computing enables them to study complex molecules like amantadine, which has pharmaceutical relevance, while their successful construction of a full 2D potential energy surface for water on quantum hardware showcases the technology's growing capability to map chemical landscapes. Most importantly, the work shows that quantum-computed molecular energies are now achieving agreement with gold-standard classical reference calculations across multiple molecular systems, suggesting we may be approaching the threshold where quantum advantage becomes tangible for chemistry applications. The integration of advanced ansätze like LUCJ with density matrix embedding theory provides a promising roadmap for scaling quantum chemistry simulations to larger, industrially relevant molecules.

We present a large-scale experimental study of quantum-computing-based molecular simulation carried out on IQM's Sirius 24-qubit superconducting processor, utilizing up to 16 operational qubits. The work employs Sample-based Quantum Diagonalization (SQD) together with the Local Unitary Cluster Jastrow (LUCJ) ansatz to estimate ground-state energies for a set of benchmark molecules, including H$_2$, LiH, BeH$_2$, H$_2$O, and NH$_3$. In addition, we introduce a Linear-CNOT variant of the Unitary Coupled-Cluster Singles and Doubles (LCNot-UCCSD) ansatz within the SQD workflow, trading higher circuit depth for reduced classical preprocessing. A comparison between these ansätze is provided, clarifying their respective strengths, limitations, and suitability for near-term quantum hardware. We further explore potential energy landscapes through 1D scans for H$_2$ and HeH$^+$ using both STO-3G and 6-31G basis sets, and for LiH and BeH$_2$ in STO-3G. Extending beyond this, we demonstrate the experimental construction of a full 2D potential energy surface for the water molecule on quantum hardware, mapped over a 32 $\times$ 32 grid in bond length and bond angle. To move beyond small benchmark systems, we combine SQD(LUCJ) with Density Matrix Embedding Theory (DMET) to compute active-space energies for a set of ligand-like molecules, as well as the pharmacologically relevant amantadine system. Across all studies, the majority of quantum-computed energies agree with reference FCI results, as well as with DMET-CASCI energies for embedded systems, to within chemical accuracy for the chosen basis sets. These results demonstrate the reliability of sample-based diagonalization approaches and underscore the potential of hybrid embedding strategies for extending quantum simulations to increasingly complex molecular systems, while also highlighting their practicality on current IQM quantum hardware.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quip.Network Launches Quantum-Classical Blockchain Testnet Built in Consultation with D-Wave
item.description:
Curator's Take: This testnet represents a significant step toward quantum-resistant blockchain infrastructure, addressing one of the most pressing challenges facing cryptocurrency and distributed ledger systems as quantum computers advance. The collaboration with D-Wave, a leader in quantum annealing systems, suggests they're leveraging real quantum hardware insights rather than purely theoretical approaches to build defenses against future quantum threats. With over 13,000 participants already signed up, this testnet could provide valuable real-world data on how quantum-classical hybrid systems perform under diverse conditions, potentially informing the development of post-quantum cryptographic standards. The timing is particularly relevant as NIST recently finalized its post-quantum cryptography standards, making practical implementations like this crucial for understanding how these new security measures work in practice.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Postquant Labs, the developer building Quip.Network, today announced the launch of the first publicly available quantum-classical blockchain testnet, built in consultation with D-Wave Quantum Inc. (“D-Wave”), the world’s leading quantum computing company. More than 13,000 people have signed up to participate in the testnet, which enables the global research community […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Networking Fundamentals: From Physical Protocols to Network Engineering
item.description:
Curator's Take: This comprehensive tutorial tackles one of quantum networking's most pressing challenges: bridging the gap between promising lab demonstrations and the engineering realities of building a scalable Quantum Internet. The authors introduce Software-Defined Quantum Networking (SDQN) as a crucial framework for managing the unique constraints of quantum resources, much like how software-defined networking revolutionized classical networks by separating control from data planes. Their focus on the "simulation-reality gap" addresses a critical blind spot in current quantum network research, where idealized simulations often ignore the messy realities of decoherence, probabilistic operations, and hardware limitations that will define real-world quantum networks. This work provides essential architectural foundations that quantum engineers will need as the field moves beyond proof-of-concept experiments toward practical, multi-user quantum network deployments.

The realization of the Quantum Internet promises transformative capabilities in secure communication, distributed quantum computing, and high-precision metrology. However, transitioning from laboratory experiments to a scalable, multi-tenant network utility introduces deep orchestration challenges. Current development is often siloed within physics communities, prioritizing hardware, while the classical networking community lacks architectural models to manage fragile quantum resources. This tutorial bridges this divide by providing a network-centric view of quantum networking. We dismantle idealized assumptions in current simulators to address the "simulation-reality gap," recasting them as explicit control-plane constraints. To bridge this gap, we establish Software-Defined Quantum Networking (SDQN) as a prerequisite for scale, prioritizing a symbiotic, dual-plane architecture where classical control dictates quantum data flow. Specifically, we synthesize reference models for SDQN and the Quantum Network Operating System (QNOS) for hardware abstraction, and adapt a Quantum Network Utility Maximization (Q-NUM) framework as a unifying mathematical lens for engineers to reason about trade-offs between entanglement routing, scheduling, and fidelity. Furthermore, we analyze Distributed Quantum AI (DQAI) over imperfect networks as a case study, illustrating how physical constraints such as probabilistic stragglers and decoherence dictate application-layer viability. Ultimately, this tutorial equips network engineers with the tools required to transition quantum networking from a bespoke physics experiment into a programmable, multi-tenant global infrastructure.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Topology-Hiding Connectivity-Assurance for QKD Inter-Networking
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a critical vulnerability in quantum key distribution networks that could undermine their security promises in practice. While QKD provides theoretically unbreakable encryption between two points, real-world quantum networks rely on trusted repeaters and complex topologies that create potential attack vectors - essentially, you have to trust the network infrastructure itself. The proposed topology-hiding protocol offers an elegant solution by allowing network operators to cryptographically prove they can deliver secure quantum connections without revealing sensitive details about their network architecture, which could otherwise be exploited by adversaries. This work represents an important step toward making large-scale quantum communication networks both practically deployable and genuinely trustworthy, addressing the gap between QKD's theoretical security guarantees and the messy realities of network infrastructure.

While QKD ensures information-theoretic security at the link level, real-world deployments depend on trusted repeaters, creating potential vulnerabilities. In this paper, we thus introduce a topology-hiding connectivity assurance protocol to enhance trust in quantum key distribution (QKD) network infrastructures. Our protocol allows network providers to jointly prove the existence of a secure connection between endpoints without revealing internal topology details. By extending graph-signature techniques to support multi-graphs and hidden endpoints, we enable zero-knowledge proofs of connectivity that ensure both soundness and topology hiding. We further discuss how our approach can certify, e.g., multiple disjoint paths, supporting multi-path QKD scenarios. This work bridges cryptographic assurance methods with the operational requirements of QKD networks, promoting verifiable and privacy-preserving inter-network connectivity.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Topology-Hiding Path Validation for Large-Scale Quantum Key Distribution Networks
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles a critical trust problem that could make or break large-scale quantum key distribution networks: how do you verify that your quantum-secured communication is actually following the promised path without revealing sensitive network topology to potential adversaries? The researchers have developed a clever protocol that lets users verify their QKD traffic is using certified, properly configured nodes and following agreed-upon routes, while keeping the network operator's infrastructure details confidential. With computational costs under 2.5 seconds and minimal communication overhead for 100-node networks, this work addresses a real-world deployment challenge that quantum networks will face as they scale beyond laboratory demonstrations. This kind of practical security infrastructure development is exactly what's needed to move quantum communication from promising technology to trustworthy commercial reality.

Secure long-distance communication in quantum key distribution (QKD) networks depends on trusted repeater nodes along the entire transmission path. Consequently, these nodes will be subject to strict auditing and certification in future large-scale QKD deployments. However, trust must also extend to the network operator, who is responsible for fulfilling contractual obligations -- such as ensuring certified devices are used and transmission paths remain disjoint where required. In this work, we present a path validation protocol specifically designed for QKD networks. It enables the receiver to verify compliance with agreed-upon policies. At the same time, the protocol preserves the operator's confidentiality by ensuring that no sensitive information about the network topology is revealed to users. We provide a formal model and a provably secure generic construction of the protocol, along with a concrete instantiation. For long-distance communication involving 100 nodes, the protocol has a computational cost of 1-2.5s depending on the machine, and a communication overhead of less than 70kB - demonstrating the efficiency of our approach.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Novel approach to quantum error correction portends a scalable future for quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough tackles one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: the enormous overhead required for error correction, where hundreds or thousands of physical qubits are typically needed to create just one logical qubit capable of reliable computation. Dr. Williamson's novel "gauging" approach could dramatically reduce this ratio, potentially accelerating the timeline for practical, large-scale quantum computers by making fault-tolerant systems more resource-efficient. The work represents a significant theoretical advance that could reshape how the field approaches the scalability problem, offering a more viable path toward quantum computers with millions of logical qubits needed for transformative applications. Published in Nature Physics, this research provides crucial insights that hardware developers across the industry will likely incorporate into their roadmaps for building commercially viable quantum systems.

A University of Sydney quantum physicist has developed a new approach to quantum error correction that could significantly reduce the number of physical qubits required to build large-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computers. The study, co-authored by Dr. Dominic Williamson from the School of Physics, is titled "Low-overhead fault-tolerant quantum computation by gauging logical operators" and published in Nature Physics.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Distribution of Bell State Entanglement in Qubit Networks via Collision Models
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents an elegant solution to one of quantum networking's fundamental challenges: how to efficiently distribute entanglement between qubits that don't directly interact with each other. The researchers' collision model approach uses environmental ancilla qubits as "quantum messengers" that can carry entanglement between distant nodes in a network, even creating maximally entangled Bell pairs between non-neighboring qubits through carefully tuned interactions. What makes this particularly compelling is the introduction of a "repeated interaction model" where the ancilla retains information between interactions, potentially offering more efficient entanglement distribution than traditional reset-based collision models. This work provides a practical framework that could significantly advance modular quantum computing architectures and distributed quantum sensing networks, where establishing entanglement between separated quantum processors remains a critical bottleneck.

We propose a general scheme to controllably distribute pairwise entanglement in a quantum network of qubits by exploiting environmental ancilla qubits interacting with the network nodes through tunable Hamiltonians. Our approach leverages collision models, in which a quantum syatem interacts sequentially with ancilla units. We explore two distinct scenarios within this framework: one in which the ancilla is reset to its initial coherent state after each interaction (the traditional collision model), and another where the ancilla is not reset but its state is simply carried over to the next interaction, which we dub the repeated interaction model. In both scenarios, we ensure that the system-ancilla correlations are discarded between steps. We also demonstrate how varying the ancilla-system interaction patterns enables selective generation of entanglement between different qubit pairs, including non-neighbouring nodes that do not directly interact. The scheme is analyzed in networks up to three qubits under both collision and repeated interaction dynamics, revealing the genaration of maximally entangled bell pairs even in configurations where the interacting ancilla couples to only a single node. Our results provide a systematic and physically implementable route to entanglement distribution, offering potential applications in quantum communication, metrology and modular quantum computing.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Trapped ion quantum technology gets smaller
item.description:
Curator's Take: This development represents a crucial step toward making quantum technologies practical for real-world deployment, as trapped ion systems have traditionally required large, laboratory-scale setups with complex laser cooling and control systems. The miniaturization of trapped ion technology could accelerate the transition from proof-of-concept quantum devices to portable quantum sensors, field-deployable atomic clocks, and eventually compact quantum computers that don't need room-sized infrastructure. While the article doesn't specify the exact scale of miniaturization achieved, any progress in making trapped ion systems more compact is significant given that these platforms offer some of the highest-fidelity quantum operations demonstrated to date. This advancement could particularly impact quantum sensing applications where portability is essential, such as navigation systems that don't rely on GPS or precision measurements in remote locations.

Portable device could find applications in quantum computing and optical clocks The post Trapped ion quantum technology gets smaller appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum-Enhanced Processing with Tensor-Network Frontends for Privacy-Aware Federated Medical Diagnosis
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum computing's most pressing challenges: how to meaningfully apply quantum processing to real-world problems when current quantum computers have severe limitations on qubit count and noise levels. The researchers cleverly sidestep the "small quantum computer, big data" problem by using tensor networks to compress medical images into compact representations that quantum processors can actually handle, while maintaining privacy through secure multi-party computation. What's particularly noteworthy is their finding that the quantum enhancement effect depends heavily on the specific tensor network architecture used, suggesting that the choice of classical preprocessing isn't just about compression but fundamentally shapes how quantum advantages can emerge. This hybrid approach represents a pragmatic pathway for near-term quantum applications in healthcare, where privacy constraints and data complexity have made quantum machine learning seem impossibly distant from practical deployment.

We propose a privacy-aware hybrid framework for federated medical image classification that combines tensor-network representation learning, MPC-secured aggregation, and post-aggregation quantum refinement. The framework is motivated by two practical constraints in privacy-aware federated learning: MPC can introduce substantial communication overhead, and direct quantum processing of high-dimensional medical images is unrealistic with a small number of qubits. To address both constraints within a single architecture, client-side tensor-network frontends, Matrix Product State (MPS), Tree Tensor Network (TTN), and Multi-scale Entanglement Renormalization Ansatz (MERA), compress local inputs into compact latent representations, after which a Quantum-Enhanced Processor (QEP) refines the aggregated latent feature through quantum-state embedding and observable-based readout. Experiments on PneumoniaMNIST show that the effect of the QEP is frontend-dependent rather than uniform across architectures. In the present setting, the TTN+QEP combination exhibits the most balanced overall profile. The results also suggest that the QEP behaves more stably when the qubit count is sufficiently matched to the latent dimension, while noisy conditions degrade performance relative to the noiseless setting. The MPC benchmark further shows that communication cost is governed primarily by the dimension of the protected latent representation. This indicates that tensor-network compression plays a dual role: it enables small-qubit quantum processing on compressed latent features and reduces the communication overhead associated with secure aggregation. Taken together, these results support a co-design perspective in which representation compression, post-aggregation quantum refinement, and privacy-aware deployment should be optimized jointly.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Distributed Variational Quantum Linear Solver
item.description:
Curator's Take: This paper tackles one of the most fundamental bottlenecks in quantum computing: how to solve problems larger than what a single quantum device can handle. By developing a distributed approach that lets multiple NISQ computers work together on different pieces of a linear equation system, the researchers are essentially creating a pathway for quantum networks to tackle real-world problems that would otherwise be impossible on today's limited hardware. The work is particularly significant because linear equation solving underlies countless applications from machine learning to scientific simulation, and this distributed framework could unlock quantum advantages for problems that are currently beyond reach. While still in the simulation phase, this research represents crucial groundwork for the eventual quantum internet era, where networked quantum computers will need to collaborate seamlessly on complex computational tasks.

This paper develops a distributed variational quantum algorithm for solving large-scale linear equations. For a linear system of the form $Ax=b$, the large square matrix $A$ is partitioned into smaller square block submatrices, each of which is known only to a single noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) computer. Each NISQ computer communicates with certain other quantum computers in the same row and column of the block partition, where the communication patterns are described by the row- and column-neighbor graphs, both of which are connected. The proposed algorithm integrates a variant of the variational quantum linear solver at each computer with distributed classical optimization techniques. The derivation of the quantum cost function provides insight into the design of the distributed algorithm. Numerical quantum simulations demonstrate that the proposed distributed quantum algorithm can solve linear systems whose size scales with the number of computers and is therefore not limited by the capacity of a single quantum computer.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Useful quantum computers could be built with as few as 10,000 qubits, team finds
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough from Caltech and Oratomic represents a dramatic shift in quantum computing timelines, potentially bringing practical quantum computers decades closer to reality by slashing the required qubit count from millions to just tens of thousands. The research tackles one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges—error correction—through innovative approaches that could make fault-tolerant quantum systems feasible with today's emerging hardware capabilities. While current quantum computers from IBM, Google, and others already boast hundreds to thousands of qubits, this work suggests we may be tantalizingly close to the threshold where quantum advantage becomes truly transformative for real-world applications. If validated, this could accelerate the race toward commercially viable quantum computers that outperform classical systems on practically important problems within this decade.

Quantum computers of the future may be closer to reality thanks to new research from Caltech and Oratomic, a Caltech-linked start-up company. Theorists and experimentalists teamed up to develop a new approach for reducing the errors that riddle today's rudimentary quantum computers. Whereas these machines were previously thought to require millions of qubits to work properly (qubits being the quantum equivalent to 1's and 0's in classical computers), the new results indicate that a fully realized quantum computer could be built with as few as 10,000 to 20,000 qubits. The need for fewer qubits means that quantum computers could, in theory, be operational by the end of the decade.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: EPB Joins Southeastern Quantum Collaborative to Support Regional Infrastructure Integration
item.description:
Curator's Take: This development highlights how quantum computing is transitioning from isolated research efforts to coordinated regional ecosystems that bring together universities, major quantum companies like IBM and IonQ, and critical infrastructure providers. EPB's participation is particularly noteworthy given their expertise in advanced networking infrastructure, which will be essential for connecting quantum systems across the Southeast and potentially enabling distributed quantum computing architectures. The Southeastern Quantum Collaborative represents a strategic approach to building the foundational infrastructure and workforce needed to compete in the quantum race, following successful models like the Chicago Quantum Network. This type of regional coordination could accelerate practical quantum deployments by ensuring the necessary telecommunications backbone and skilled workforce are developed in parallel with the quantum hardware itself.

EPB has joined the Southeastern Quantum Collaborative (SQC) as an inaugural member. Led by the University of Alabama in Huntsville (UAH), the SQC is a regional association of academic, industrial, and government organizations—including IBM, D-Wave, and IonQ—tasked with coordinating quantum information science research and workforce development across the Southeast. EPB’s role in the collaborative involves [...] The post EPB Joins Southeastern Quantum Collaborative to Support Regional Infrastructure Integration appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Silicon quantum computer performs logical operations for the first time
item.description:
Curator's Take: This milestone represents a crucial bridge between classical and quantum computing, as silicon-based logical operations could leverage the existing semiconductor manufacturing infrastructure that powers today's electronics industry. The achievement is particularly significant because logical qubits, which use quantum error correction to maintain coherence, are essential for building fault-tolerant quantum computers that can run practical algorithms. Silicon's compatibility with established fabrication techniques and its demonstrated long coherence times position it as a strong contender against other quantum computing platforms like superconducting circuits and trapped ions. This breakthrough brings us closer to scalable quantum computers that could be manufactured using modified versions of existing chip foundries, potentially accelerating the timeline for commercially viable quantum computing.

Silicon is ubiquitous in modern electronics, and now it is becoming increasingly useful in quantum computing. In particular, silicon's compatibility with existing chip technology and its long coherence times in silicon-based spin qubits make it a promising material for scalable quantum computing. A new study, published in Nature Nanotechnology, has demonstrated silicon's use in a logical quantum processor, representing the first of its kind.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum computer accurately simulates real magnetic materials, reproducing national laboratory data
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article marks a significant milestone where quantum computers have moved beyond toy problems to accurately reproduce real experimental data from national laboratories, demonstrating genuine utility in materials science applications. The ability to simulate magnetic materials with quantum hardware addresses one of the most promising near-term use cases for quantum computing, as these quantum many-body systems are notoriously difficult for classical computers to handle accurately. This validation against established experimental benchmarks suggests we're approaching the threshold where quantum simulators could begin providing practical insights for materials discovery and design, potentially accelerating the development of everything from better batteries to novel superconductors. The achievement represents a crucial step toward quantum advantage in scientifically relevant problems rather than abstract mathematical demonstrations.

Studying and designing novel materials is a central application of quantum mechanics. Chemists, materials scientists, and physicists focus on subtle interactions in quantum materials and to uncover them they rely on sophisticated computational and experimental techniques. Computer simulations that connect microscopic quantum interactions to measurable material properties complement experimental data to connect structure to function—but classical computers can struggle to simulate those properties. Fortunately, scientists today have a new tool in their toolbox: quantum computers.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Unlocking scalable entanglement will enable next-generation quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: creating and maintaining entanglement at scale, which is essential for quantum computers to achieve their promised computational advantages over classical systems. While the summary presents familiar quantum computing benefits, the focus on scalable entanglement addresses a critical bottleneck that has limited current quantum computers to small problem sizes and specific use cases. Successfully scaling entanglement across hundreds or thousands of qubits would mark a pivotal transition from today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices to fault-tolerant quantum computers capable of solving real-world problems. This represents a key engineering frontier where breakthroughs could dramatically accelerate the timeline for practical quantum advantage across multiple industries.

Quantum computing promises to transform our world in rapid, radical and revolutionary ways: solving in seconds problems that would take classical computers years, accelerating the discovery of new medicines, creating sustainable materials, optimizing complex systems, and strengthening cybersecurity. It does so using qubits, the quantum counterparts of classical bits, which can occupy multiple states simultaneously and enable a fundamentally new kind of computation.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Dual-rail superconducting qubits generate high-fidelity logical entanglement, study finds
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a significant step toward fault-tolerant quantum computing by demonstrating that dual-rail superconducting qubits can create high-fidelity logical entanglement, a crucial building block for quantum error correction. The achievement is particularly noteworthy because logical qubits, which are encoded using multiple physical qubits to protect against errors, have historically struggled with maintaining the delicate quantum entanglement needed for complex computations. By achieving high-fidelity logical entanglement in superconducting systems, this work brings us closer to practical quantum computers that can run meaningful algorithms while automatically correcting the errors that plague current noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices. The dual-rail approach offers a promising path toward scalable quantum error correction, potentially accelerating the timeline for quantum advantage in real-world applications.

Quantum computers, systems that process information leveraging quantum mechanical effects, could outperform classical computers on some advanced tasks. These systems rely on qubits, the fundamental units of quantum information, that become linked via an effect known as quantum entanglement and share a unified quantum state.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion
item.description:
Curator's Take: Google's decision to pursue both superconducting and neutral atom quantum computing platforms represents a significant strategic pivot that acknowledges the fundamental uncertainties still facing the field. This dual-modality approach hedges against the possibility that superconducting qubits alone may not achieve the scale and coherence needed for practical quantum advantage, while neutral atoms offer compelling advantages in connectivity and potential for larger qubit counts. The move follows similar multi-platform strategies from other major players like Amazon and IBM, suggesting the industry is maturing beyond single-technology bets toward more diversified research portfolios. This expansion could accelerate progress by allowing Google to leverage the unique strengths of each platform while cross-pollinating insights between their hardware teams.

Google Quantum AI has announced a major strategic expansion of its research roadmap, adding neutral atom quantum computing to its established superconducting hardware program. Led by founder Hartmut Neven, the move signals a shift toward a multi-platform approach to reach commercially relevant quantum advantage. While Google remains confident in the timeline for superconducting systems by [...] The post Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: New Zealand Researchers Report Advance in Optical Ising Machine for Hybrid Quantum Optimization
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research highlights an important trend in quantum computing where hybrid classical-quantum systems are emerging as practical stepping stones toward full-scale quantum advantage. Optical Ising machines represent a particularly promising approach because they can leverage quantum-inspired physics to tackle complex optimization problems without requiring the extreme engineering challenges of fault-tolerant quantum computers. The New Zealand team's focus on scalability is especially significant, as many current quantum and quantum-inspired systems struggle to maintain performance advantages as problem sizes grow. While we await the arrival of fully fault-tolerant quantum computers, these hybrid optimization devices could deliver real-world value in logistics, finance, and materials science much sooner than traditional quantum computing timelines suggest.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — With the world’s first scalable, fault-tolerant quantum computer expected to be years away, a group of New Zealand researchers has developed an exciting hybrid device that could solve big, intractable problems sooner. Research Fellow, Dr Liam Quinn of Te Whai Ao — Dodd-Walls Centre is leading the work, considered of […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Washington Begins to Treat Quantum Technology as a Strategic Priority
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a crucial shift as quantum technology moves from research curiosity to national security imperative in Washington's policy circles. The bipartisan engagement suggests lawmakers are beginning to understand quantum's dual nature as both an enormous economic opportunity and a potential threat to current cryptographic systems that underpin everything from financial transactions to government communications. What makes this particularly significant is the timing - as quantum computers edge closer to practical applications, the window for establishing regulatory frameworks, funding priorities, and international cooperation agreements is narrowing rapidly. The fact that these discussions are happening now, rather than after quantum advantage becomes widespread, suggests policymakers may finally be getting ahead of the technology curve for once.

Insider Brief Tuesday in Washington offered a clear picture of where quantum technology now stands, not as a future concept, but as a present responsibility. Meetings on Capitol Hill, organized by Optica, brought together lawmakers, federal agencies, and leaders from across the quantum ecosystem to focus on what comes next. The conversations were not about […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Paves a Two-Lane Quantum Roadmap by Adding Neutral Atom Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a major strategic shift for Google Quantum AI, which has built its quantum supremacy claims and current leadership position around superconducting qubits but is now hedging its bets by exploring neutral atom systems. The move reflects growing industry recognition that different quantum hardware approaches may excel at different types of problems, with neutral atoms potentially offering advantages in connectivity and scalability that could complement Google's superconducting expertise. By diversifying its quantum hardware portfolio, Google is positioning itself to capitalize on whichever technology proves most viable for near-term commercial applications, while also acknowledging that the path to fault-tolerant quantum computing may require multiple parallel approaches rather than betting everything on a single platform.

Insider Brief Google Quantum AI, which was a pioneer in the superconducting approach to quantum computing, announced that it is expanding beyond that core approach to add neutral atom quantum computing, a move the company says will accelerate progress toward commercially useful machines. The plan, discussed in a recent Google Quantum AI blog post, would […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Scalable quantum circuit generation for iterative ground state approximation using Majorana Propagation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles one of quantum computing's most promising near-term applications - finding ground states of molecular systems - by cleverly combining classical preprocessing with quantum circuit design. The researchers demonstrate their ADAPT-VMPE algorithm can generate practical quantum circuits for systems as large as 100 qubits while maintaining polynomial scaling, which is crucial for making quantum chemistry simulations feasible on near-term devices. What makes this particularly exciting is their focus on a real-world photosensitizer molecule used in cancer treatment, showing how quantum algorithms could eventually accelerate drug discovery and materials science. The theoretical guarantees on approximation error and the polynomial complexity bounds suggest this approach could bridge the gap between today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and useful quantum advantage in molecular simulation.
